title: 11 Books to Read This July
gist: Nautilus's July reading roundup clusters around recurring 2026 zeitgeist threads: data as a historical power lever and hyperscale data centers as a privacy flashpoint (Data Empire, Cloudthief), biosecurity and elite-survivalist dystopias (Biological War, The Felicity Complex), a philosophical rebuttal of Mars/Moon-colonization promises (Lunacy), and reproductive-biology trivia paired with climate-restoration hope (On the Origin of Sex, The Earth Said Remember Me).

insights.claim: Roopika Risam's book "Data Empire" argues data has functioned as a form of power throughout human history — noting the first human name ever recorded in writing belonged to an accountant — and that today's data-power brokers are rapidly shifting.
insights.speaker: Liz Greene
insights.so_what: Frames data control and ownership as a long-run power axis — relevant thematic backdrop for debates on data-infrastructure and AI-compute concentration.
insights.quote: The first human name recorded in written history was an accountant.
insights.locator: Data Empire: The Power of Information to Organize, Control and Dominate

insights.claim: Nathaniel Rich's heist novel "Cloudthief" follows two characters attempting to rob a data center "the size of a small city" that holds the world's public and private information, using the premise to interrogate the collapse of privacy in a data-saturated society.
insights.speaker: Liz Greene
insights.so_what: Fiction treating hyperscale data centers as the era's ultimate physical treasure vault is a cultural signal of how central data-center infrastructure has become to public imagination, paralleling real-world AI-datacenter buildout narratives.
insights.quote: a data center, the size of a small city, that contains all the world's public and private information

insights.claim: Annie Jacobsen's "Biological War: A Scenario" models a hypothetical outbreak from a leaked deadly disease, grounding the scenario in dozens of interviews with biosecurity experts rather than pure fiction.
insights.speaker: Liz Greene
insights.so_what: Signals continued public and expert appetite for biosecurity risk scenarios post-COVID — a genre indicator relevant to biodefense-adjacent policy and publishing narratives.
insights.quote: the information is based on dozens of interviews with experts, painting a chilling picture of a disaster that’s all too possible
insights.locator: Biological War: A Scenario by Annie Jacobson

insights.claim: August Clarke's dystopian novel "The Felicity Complex" depicts lab-grown women created to comfort wealthy survivalists sheltering in a luxury fallout bunker, who eventually rebel against their programming.
insights.speaker: Liz Greene
insights.so_what: Reflects a recurring cultural anxiety theme — elite survivalist bunkers and engineered servitude — in current speculative fiction, a soft signal for the broader "apocalypse economy" narrative.
insights.quote: what happens when they rebel against their programming?
insights.locator: The Felicity Complex by August Clarke

insights.claim: Philosopher Ben Bramble's "Lunacy: Ten False Promises of the New Space Age" systematically refutes ten common claims used to justify space colonization, including that humans could simply relocate to Mars or the Moon if Earth becomes uninhabitable.
insights.speaker: Liz Greene
insights.so_what: A direct contrarian counter-narrative to the Mars/Moon-colonization investment thesis promoted by space companies — useful as a skeptic's-case reference when evaluating space-settlement rhetoric.
insights.quote: We should explore space for curiosity’s sake, not as a half-baked back-up plan.
insights.locator: Lunacy: Ten False Promises of the New Space Age

insights.claim: Lixing Sun's "On the Origin of Sex" catalogs extreme variation in biological reproduction, noting California condors can reproduce without fertilized eggs and slime molds exhibit hundreds of proto-sexes.
insights.speaker: Liz Greene
insights.so_what: Illustrates the counter-intuitive, shareable science trivia that drives pop-science book sales — a genre-strength signal for publishers like Hachette Book Group.
insights.quote: California condors are capable of reproducing without fertilized eggs
insights.locator: On the Origin of Sex: The Weird and Wonderful Science of Reproduction

insights.claim: Jason Dove Mark's "The Earth Said Remember Me" pairs memory of pre-climate-change ecosystems with active planetary restoration, framing remembrance itself as a motivational tool for climate action rather than pure doom-messaging.
insights.speaker: Liz Greene
insights.so_what: Represents a climate-hope/restoration-narrative genre gaining publishing traction as pure doom-messaging fatigue sets in — a soft indicator for climate-adjacent media and behavior-change campaigns.
insights.locator: The Earth Said Remember Me: How to Revive Our Memories and Restore the Planet
